## 1. The Compression Hierarchy {#sec-compression-hierarchy}

Data, information, and knowledge are not synonyms. Data is raw signal. Information is data given structure and context. Knowledge is information integrated by an agent — tested, related, made available for inference and action. The leap from information to knowledge requires compression: not merely reducing bits, but reorganising what is retained so that it supports progressively richer use.

This chapter distinguishes three levels of compression that the remainder of the paper will track across transformer architectures, the autoregressive loop, and the broader knowledge ecology. The levels differ not in degree but in kind — in what they treat as worth preserving and what they are willing to discard.

### Algorithmic compression {#sec-algorithmic-compression}

Classical algorithmic compression — Huffman coding, Lempel-Ziv, arithmetic coding — finds and exploits statistical redundancy in a signal. It operates on surface structure: repeated subsequences, character frequency distributions, local correlations. It reduces bits, often losslessly, and it does not generalise. A gzip model of English text has learned nothing about English. It has learned which byte sequences recur in a particular corpus. Applied to a new corpus with different statistics, it starts from scratch.

This is compression in the narrowest sense. It is also the sense most people mean when they use the word, which is why the broader usage requires establishing what lies beyond it.

### Organisational compression {#sec-organisational-compression}

Organisational compression discovers structure. Rather than encoding which patterns recur, it encodes the *shape* of the space that generates the patterns — building a model from which the regularities follow rather than cataloguing the regularities themselves.

A grammar is organisational compression: a finite set of rules that generates an unbounded set of valid sentences. A taxonomy is organisational compression: it encodes structural relationships from which the properties of individual instances can be partly inferred. The difference between memorising every chess game ever played and learning the principles of chess is the difference between algorithmic and organisational compression. The latter generalises to positions never seen before, because it has captured something about the structure of the domain rather than the surface of the data.

### Semantic compression {#sec-semantic-compression}

Semantic compression goes further. Where organisational compression captures structure, semantic compression builds a *navigable meaning geometry* — encoding not just how symbols relate syntactically but how concepts relate in a space where proximity, direction, and distance carry meaning.

In a well-formed semantic representation, concepts that are related are geometrically proximate. Analogical relationships are preserved as structural regularities — the classic finding that vector arithmetic in word embedding spaces captures semantic relations [@mikolov_etal_2013] is an elementary example of what, at scale, becomes a rich geometry supporting inference through navigation. This is the compression that transformer-based language models perform, and it is qualitatively distinct from anything achievable by algorithmic means. The model has not merely found patterns in text. It has built an internal space in which meaning has geometric structure, and that structure supports generalisation, composition, and inference that go beyond any specific training example.

The hierarchy is accumulative, not sequential. Semantic compression does not replace organisational compression; it presupposes and extends it. A system performing semantic compression is necessarily also performing organisational compression — it must have discovered the structural regularities before it can encode their meaning relationships. And organisational compression operates on input that has already been algorithmically compressed into a tractable form. Each level operates on the output of the level below.

### Rate-distortion as the natural formalism {#sec-rate-distortion-hierarchy}

What makes this a hierarchy rather than a list is that the three levels differ in what they count as acceptable loss. Rate-distortion theory [@shannon_1959] provides the formal framework: the minimum rate (bits) required to encode a source at a given level of distortion depends entirely on the distortion measure — the function that specifies what counts as loss.

This is Shannon's deepest insight for the present framework: the distortion measure is a *choice*. It encodes what the compressor considers worth preserving.

At the algorithmic level, distortion is literal signal error — dropped bits, corrupted bytes, mismatched characters. Acceptable loss is zero or near-zero; the goal is faithful reproduction. At the organisational level, distortion is structural misrepresentation — a model that assigns the wrong category, a grammar that generates invalid sentences, a set of principles that leads to losing positions. Surface fidelity to specific instances is freely sacrificed; what must be preserved is the structure that generates them. At the semantic level, distortion is meaning failure — representations that place unrelated concepts nearby, that destroy analogical structure, that support invalid inferences. Surface form and even structural detail can be discarded; what must be preserved is the geometry of meaning.

Each level defines a different rate-distortion problem, with a progressively more abstract relevance criterion determining what counts as loss. This is what makes the hierarchy generative rather than merely descriptive: each deeper level of compression can tolerate more surface distortion precisely because it is preserving something more abstract, and what it preserves supports richer downstream use. A system that has achieved only algorithmic compression can reproduce; one that has achieved organisational compression can generalise; one that has achieved semantic compression can reason by navigation.

@tishby_zaslavsky_2015 provide the bridge to deep learning: each layer of a deep network can be understood as solving a rate-distortion problem, compressing its input while preserving information relevant to the task. @achille_soatto_2018 show that the resulting minimal sufficient statistics naturally produce invariant and disentangled representations — representations with structure that goes beyond what mutual information with the input captures. The connection to the hierarchy is direct: deeper layers solve rate-distortion problems with progressively more abstract relevance criteria, and what emerges at each level is not just preserved information but new organisation of that information. @sec-structure-across-depth will make this precise.

The hierarchy is not substrate-specific. Any system that must compress knowledge for transmission through a bandwidth-limited channel — biological or artificial — faces the same nested sequence of rate-distortion problems. @sec-convergence will develop this claim with the biological evidence. For now, the point is that the hierarchy describes a property of the *problem*, not of any particular architecture. The transformer is one implementation. The question the framework addresses is what follows from taking hierarchical compression seriously as the central operation these systems perform.
